title: DM$^3$-Nav: Decentralized Multi-Agent Multimodal Multi-Object Semantic Navigation
summary: We present DM$^3$-Nav, a fully decentralized multi-agent semantic navigation system supporting multimodal open-vocabulary goal specification and multi-object missions. In our setting, decentralization implies operation without a central coordinator, global map aggregation, or shared global state at runtime. Robots operate autonomously and coordinate through ad-hoc pairwise communication, exchanging local maps, goal status, and navigation intent without synchronization. An implicit task allocation mechanism combining intent broadcasting and distance-weighted frontier selection reduces redundant exploration while preserving decentralized operation. Evaluations on HM3DSem scenes using the HM3Dv0.2 and GOAT-Bench datasets demonstrate that DM$^3$-Nav matches or exceeds centralized and shared-map baselines while eliminating single points of failure inherent in centralized architectures. Finally, we validate our approach in a real-world office environment using two mobile robots, demonstrating successful deployment relying entirely on onboard sensing and computation. A video of our real-world experiments is available online: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1QiUSCn5rIvtuTUqtuXLPgmt6S8x9-MCZ/view?usp=drive_link

AI.tldr: DM³-Nav is a decentralized multi-agent semantic navigation system that enables robots to autonomously explore and navigate using only local sensing and pairwise communication, supporting multimodal open-vocabulary goals and multi-object missions without centralized coordination.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent navigation systems often rely on centralized coordination, global maps, or shared global states, which create single points of failure and synchronization bottlenecks. The authors aim to develop a fully decentralized approach that eliminates these dependencies while maintaining or improving performance.
AI.method: The system operates without central coordination, global map aggregation, or shared global state. Robots operate autonomously using local sensing and coordinate through ad-hoc pairwise communication, exchanging local maps, goal status, and navigation intent without synchronization. An implicit task allocation mechanism combines intent broadcasting and distance-weighted frontier selection to reduce redundant exploration while preserving decentralization.
AI.result: Evaluations on HM3DSem scenes using HM3Dv0.2 and GOAT-Bench datasets show that DM³-Nav matches or exceeds the performance of centralized and shared-map baselines while eliminating single points of failure. Real-world validation in an office environment with two mobile robots demonstrates successful deployment using entirely onboard sensing and computation.
AI.conclusion: DM³-Nav successfully demonstrates that fully decentralized multi-agent semantic navigation is feasible and competitive with centralized approaches. The system provides robustness by eliminating single points of failure while supporting complex multimodal goal specifications and multi-object missions, enabling practical real-world deployment with onboard sensing and computation.

title: Seeing the Whole Elephant: A Benchmark for Failure Attribution in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Failure attribution, i.e., identifying the responsible agent and decisive step of a failure, is particularly challenging in LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) due to their natural-language reasoning, nondeterministic outputs, and intricate interaction dynamics. A reliable benchmark is therefore essential to guide and evaluate attribution techniques. Yet existing benchmarks rely on partially observable traces that capture only agent outputs, omitting the inputs and context that developers actually use when debugging. We argue that failure attribution should be studied under full execution observability, aligning with real-world developer-facing scenarios where complete traces, rather than only outputs, are accessible for diagnosis. To this end, we introduce TraceElephant, a benchmark designed for failure attribution with full execution traces and reproducible environments. We then systematically evaluate failure attribution techniques across various configurations. Specifically, full traces improve attribution accuracy by up to 76\% over a partial-observation counterpart, confirming that missing inputs obscure many failure causes. TraceElephant provides a foundation for follow-up failure attribution research, promoting evaluation practices that reflect real-world debugging and supporting the development of more transparent MASs.

AI.tldr: TraceElephant: A benchmark for failure attribution in LLM-based multi-agent systems that provides full execution traces (inputs and context) rather than just outputs, improving attribution accuracy by up to 76%.
AI.motivation: Existing failure attribution benchmarks for multi-agent systems rely on partially observable traces that only capture agent outputs, omitting the inputs and context that developers actually use when debugging. This creates a mismatch with real-world scenarios where complete traces are accessible for diagnosis.
AI.method: The authors introduce TraceElephant, a benchmark designed specifically for failure attribution with full execution traces and reproducible environments. They systematically evaluate various failure attribution techniques across different configurations, comparing full trace observability versus partial observation.
AI.result: Full execution traces improve failure attribution accuracy by up to 76% compared to partial-observation counterparts. This confirms that missing inputs obscure many failure causes in LLM-based multi-agent systems.
AI.conclusion: TraceElephant provides a foundational benchmark for failure attribution research that aligns with real-world debugging scenarios. It promotes evaluation practices that reflect actual developer workflows and supports the development of more transparent and debuggable multi-agent systems.

title: An Artifact-based Agent Framework for Adaptive and Reproducible Medical Image Processing
summary: Medical imaging research is increasingly shifting from controlled benchmark evaluation toward real-world clinical deployment. In such settings, applying analytical methods extends beyond model design to require dataset-aware workflow configuration and provenance tracking. Two requirements therefore become central: \textbf{adaptability}, the ability to configure workflows according to dataset-specific conditions and evolving analytical goals; and \textbf{reproducibility}, the guarantee that all transformations and decisions are explicitly recorded and re-executable. Here, we present an artifact-based agent framework that introduces a semantic layer to augment medical image processing. The framework formalizes intermediate and final outputs through an artifact contract, enabling structured interrogation of workflow state and goal-conditioned assembly of configurations from a modular rule library. Execution is delegated to a workflow executor to preserve deterministic computational graph construction and provenance tracking, while the agent operates locally to comply with most privacy constraints. We evaluate the framework on real-world clinical CT and MRI cohorts, demonstrating adaptive configuration synthesis, deterministic reproducibility across repeated executions, and artifact-grounded semantic querying. These results show that adaptive workflow configuration can be achieved without compromising reproducibility in heterogeneous clinical environments.

AI.tldr: A semantic agent framework for medical imaging workflows that combines adaptability and reproducibility through artifact contracts and rule-based configuration.
AI.motivation: Medical imaging research is shifting from controlled benchmarks to real-world clinical deployment, requiring both adaptability (to handle dataset-specific conditions and evolving goals) and reproducibility (with explicit provenance tracking).
AI.method: An artifact-based agent framework with a semantic layer that formalizes outputs through artifact contracts, enabling structured interrogation of workflow state. It uses goal-conditioned assembly of configurations from a modular rule library, with execution delegated to a workflow executor for deterministic computational graphs and provenance tracking while maintaining local operation for privacy compliance.
AI.result: The framework was evaluated on real-world clinical CT and MRI cohorts, demonstrating adaptive configuration synthesis, deterministic reproducibility across repeated executions, and artifact-grounded semantic querying.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive workflow configuration can be achieved without compromising reproducibility in heterogeneous clinical environments, bridging the gap between flexibility and reliability in medical image processing.

title: MolClaw: An Autonomous Agent with Hierarchical Skills for Drug Molecule Evaluation, Screening, and Optimization
comment: 59 pages, 10 figures. Code and data will be released
summary: Computational drug discovery, particularly the complex workflows of drug molecule screening and optimization, requires orchestrating dozens of specialized tools in multi-step workflows, yet current AI agents struggle to maintain robust performance and consistently underperform in these high-complexity scenarios. Here we present MolClaw, an autonomous agent that leads drug molecule evaluation, screening, and optimization. It unifies over 30 specialized domain resources through a three-tier hierarchical skill architecture (70 skills in total) that facilitates agent long-term interaction at runtime: tool-level skills standardize atomic operations, workflow-level skills compose them into validated pipelines with quality check and reflection, and a discipline-level skill supplies scientific principles governing planning and verification across all scenarios in the field. Additionally, we introduce MolBench, a benchmark comprising molecular screening, optimization, and end-to-end discovery challenges spanning 8 to 50+ sequential tool calls. MolClaw achieves state-of-the-art performance across all metrics, and ablation studies confirm that gains concentrate on tasks that demand structured workflows while vanishing on those solvable with ad hoc scripting, establishing workflow orchestration competence as the primary capability bottleneck for AI-driven drug discovery.

AI.tldr: MolClaw is an autonomous AI agent for drug discovery that unifies over 30 specialized tools through a three-tier hierarchical skill architecture, achieving state-of-the-art performance on complex molecular screening and optimization workflows.
AI.motivation: Current AI agents struggle with complex drug discovery workflows that require orchestrating dozens of specialized tools in multi-step workflows. They maintain poor robust performance and consistently underperform in high-complexity drug molecule screening and optimization scenarios.
AI.method: MolClaw uses a three-tier hierarchical skill architecture: 1) Tool-level skills standardize atomic operations, 2) Workflow-level skills compose these into validated pipelines with quality checks and reflection, and 3) Discipline-level skill provides scientific principles for planning and verification. The system also introduces MolBench, a benchmark with molecular screening, optimization, and end-to-end discovery challenges spanning 8 to 50+ sequential tool calls.
AI.result: MolClaw achieves state-of-the-art performance across all metrics. Ablation studies show gains concentrate on tasks requiring structured workflows while vanishing on tasks solvable with ad hoc scripting, establishing workflow orchestration as the primary capability bottleneck.
AI.conclusion: Workflow orchestration competence is the primary capability bottleneck for AI-driven drug discovery, and MolClaw's hierarchical skill architecture effectively addresses this challenge by enabling robust performance in complex, multi-step drug molecule evaluation and optimization workflows.

title: Math Takes Two: A test for emergent mathematical reasoning in communication
summary: Although language models demonstrate remarkable proficiency on mathematical benchmarks, it remains unclear whether this reflects true mathematical reasoning or statistical pattern matching over learning formal syntax. Most existing evaluations rely on symbolic problems grounded in established mathematical conventions, limiting insight into the models' ability to construct abstract concepts from first principles. In this work, we propose Math Takes Two, a new benchmark designed to assess the emergence of mathematical reasoning through communication. Motivated by the hypothesis that mathematical cognition in humans co-evolved with the need for precise communication, our benchmark tests whether two agents, without prior mathematical knowledge, can develop a shared symbolic protocol to solve a visually grounded task where the use of a numerical system facilitates extrapolation. Unlike many current datasets, our benchmark eschews predefined mathematical language, instead requiring agents to discover latent structure and representations from scratch. Math Takes Two thus provides a novel lens through which to develop and evaluate models with emergent numerical reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark called "Math Takes Two" is proposed to test whether AI agents can develop shared mathematical concepts from first principles through communication, rather than just learning established symbolic conventions.
AI.motivation: Current mathematical benchmarks may only test pattern matching of existing mathematical syntax rather than true mathematical reasoning. The authors are motivated by the hypothesis that mathematical cognition in humans evolved alongside the need for precise communication, suggesting that true mathematical reasoning should emerge from collaborative discovery of latent structures.
AI.method: The authors propose "Math Takes Two" benchmark where two agents without prior mathematical knowledge must develop a shared symbolic protocol to solve a visually grounded task. The task requires agents to discover numerical systems from scratch, with the key that using a numerical system facilitates extrapolation in the task.
AI.result: The paper introduces a novel evaluation framework that moves beyond testing memorization of established mathematical conventions. The benchmark provides a way to assess whether models can truly construct abstract mathematical concepts from first principles through communication and collaboration.
AI.conclusion: Math Takes Two offers a new approach to evaluate emergent numerical reasoning capabilities in AI systems by requiring them to invent mathematical concepts through communication, similar to how human mathematical cognition may have evolved, providing deeper insight into whether models truly understand mathematics rather than just pattern matching.

title: Focus Session: Hardware and Software Techniques for Accelerating Multimodal Foundation Models
comment: Accepted at the Design, Automation and Test in Europe Conference (DATE), April 20-22, 2026 in Verona, Italy
summary: This work presents a multi-layered methodology for efficiently accelerating multimodal foundation models (MFMs). It combines hardware and software co-design of transformer blocks with an optimization pipeline that reduces computational and memory requirements. During model development, it employs performance enhancements through fine-tuning for domain-specific adaptation. Our methodology further incorporates hardware and software techniques for optimizing MFMs. Specifically, it employs MFM compression using hierarchy-aware mixed-precision quantization and structural pruning for transformer blocks and MLP channels. It also optimizes operations through speculative decoding, model cascading that routes queries through a small-to-large cascade and uses lightweight self-tests to determine when to escalate to larger models, as well as co-optimization of sequence length, visual resolution & stride, and graph-level operator fusion. To efficiently execute the model, the processing dataflow is optimized based on the underlying hardware architecture together with memory-efficient attention to meet on-chip bandwidth and latency budgets. To support this, a specialized hardware accelerator for the transformer workloads is employed, which can be developed through expert design or an LLM-aided design approach. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methodology on medical-MFMs and on code generation tasks, and conclude with extensions toward energy-efficient spiking-MFMs.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive hardware-software co-design methodology for accelerating multimodal foundation models using quantization, pruning, speculative decoding, model cascading, and specialized transformer accelerators.
AI.motivation: Multimodal foundation models (MFMs) require significant computational resources and memory, making efficient acceleration crucial for practical deployment across domains like medical imaging and code generation.
AI.method: Multi-layered approach combining: 1) MFM compression via hierarchy-aware mixed-precision quantization and structural pruning, 2) Operation optimization through speculative decoding and small-to-large model cascading with self-tests, 3) Co-optimization of sequence length, visual resolution & stride, and operator fusion, 4) Hardware-software co-design with specialized transformer accelerators using expert or LLM-aided design approaches.
AI.result: Demonstrated effectiveness on medical-MFMs and code generation tasks, showing reduced computational and memory requirements while meeting on-chip bandwidth and latency budgets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methodology provides an efficient framework for accelerating MFMs, with future extensions toward energy-efficient spiking-MFMs, enabling practical deployment of large multimodal models.

title: Fast Neural-Network Approximation of Active Target Search Under Uncertainty
summary: We address the problem of searching for an unknown number of stationary targets at unknown positions with a mobile agent. A probability hypothesis density filter is used to estimate the expected number of targets under measurement uncertainty. Existing planners, such as Active Search (AS) and its Intermittent variant (ASI), achieve accurate detection but require costly online optimization. To reduce online computation, we propose to use a convolutional neural network to approximate AS or ASI decisions through direct inference. The network is trained on AS/ASI data using a multi-channel grid that encodes target beliefs, the agent position, visitation history, and boundary information. Simulations with uniform and clustered target distributions show that the network achieves detection rates comparable to AS or ASI while reducing computation by orders of magnitude.

AI.tldr: A neural network approach reduces computation cost for multi-target search by learning to approximate optimal search decisions instead of costly online optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing planners like Active Search (AS) and Intermittent Active Search (ASI) provide accurate target detection but require expensive online optimization, making them computationally intensive for practical applications.
AI.method: Uses a convolutional neural network trained on AS/ASI data to approximate their decisions. The network takes a multi-channel grid input encoding: target beliefs, agent position, visitation history, and boundary information, enabling direct inference instead of online optimization.
AI.result: Simulations with both uniform and clustered target distributions show the network achieves detection rates comparable to AS/ASI while reducing computation by orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: Neural network approximation effectively reduces computational burden for multi-target search while maintaining detection performance, making search algorithms more practical for real-time applications.

title: Performance Anomaly Detection in Athletics: A Benchmarking System with Visual Analytics
summary: Anti-doping programs rely on biological testing to detect performance-enhancing drugs, but such testing costs over $800 per sample and is limited by short detection windows for many prohibited substances. These constraints leave large portions of athletes without regular testing, motivating complementary screening approaches that analyze routine competition results to identify suspicious performance patterns. We present a system that processes 1.6 million athletics performances from over 19,000 competitions (2010-2025) using eight detection methods ranging from statistical rules to machine learning and trajectory analysis. We validate all methods against publicly confirmed anti-doping violations to measure their effectiveness in identifying sanctioned athletes. Trajectory-based methods, which compare performances to expected career progression, achieve the best balance between detecting violations and limiting false alarms, though all methods face challenges from incomplete data and rare confirmed violations. The system provides an interactive interface for expert-driven investigation, emphasizing transparency and human judgment to support, rather than replace, established anti-doping processes.

AI.tldr: A screening system uses 1.6M athletic performances and 8 detection methods (statistical rules, ML, trajectory analysis) to identify suspicious patterns for anti-doping, with trajectory-based methods showing best performance balance.
AI.motivation: Traditional biological anti-doping testing is expensive (>$800 per sample) and has short detection windows, leaving many athletes untested. There's a need for complementary screening approaches using competition results to identify suspicious performance patterns.
AI.method: Processed 1.6 million athletics performances from 19,000+ competitions (2010-2025) using eight detection methods: statistical rules, machine learning, and trajectory analysis. Validated all methods against publicly confirmed anti-doping violations. Developed interactive interface for expert-driven investigation.
AI.result: Trajectory-based methods, which compare performances to expected career progression, achieved the best balance between detecting violations and limiting false alarms. All methods faced challenges from incomplete data and rare confirmed violations.
AI.conclusion: The system provides a complementary screening approach that emphasizes transparency and human judgment to support, rather than replace, established anti-doping processes, with trajectory analysis being the most effective method.

title: AgentSearchBench: A Benchmark for AI Agent Search in the Wild
summary: The rapid growth of AI agent ecosystems is transforming how complex tasks are delegated and executed, creating a new challenge of identifying suitable agents for a given task. Unlike traditional tools, agent capabilities are often compositional and execution-dependent, making them difficult to assess from textual descriptions alone. However, existing research and benchmarks typically assume well-specified functionalities, controlled candidate pools, or only executable task queries, leaving realistic agent search scenarios insufficiently studied. We introduce AgentSearchBench, a large-scale benchmark for agent search in the wild, built from nearly 10,000 real-world agents across multiple providers. The benchmark formalizes agent search as retrieval and reranking problems under both executable task queries and high-level task descriptions, and evaluates relevance using execution-grounded performance signals. Experiments reveal a consistent gap between semantic similarity and actual agent performance, exposing the limitations of description-based retrieval and reranking methods. We further show that lightweight behavioral signals, including execution-aware probing, can substantially improve ranking quality, highlighting the importance of incorporating execution signals into agent discovery. Our code is available at https://github.com/Bingo-W/AgentSearchBench.

AI.tldr: AgentSearchBench is a benchmark for realistic agent search using 10k real-world agents, showing that semantic similarity alone is insufficient and execution signals significantly improve ranking quality.
AI.motivation: AI agent ecosystems create challenges in identifying suitable agents for tasks due to their compositional and execution-dependent capabilities, which aren't well-addressed by existing benchmarks that assume well-specified functionalities or controlled candidate pools.
AI.method: Built AgentSearchBench from nearly 10,000 real-world agents across multiple providers, formalizing agent search as retrieval and reranking problems under both executable queries and high-level descriptions, with execution-grounded relevance evaluation.
AI.result: Experiments show consistent gap between semantic similarity and actual agent performance, exposing limitations of description-based methods; lightweight behavioral signals and execution-aware probing substantially improve ranking quality.
AI.conclusion: Agent discovery requires incorporating execution signals, not just semantic similarity, and AgentSearchBench provides a realistic benchmark for evaluating agent search methods in real-world scenarios.

title: Conditional anomaly detection using soft harmonic functions: An application to clinical alerting
comment: ICML 2011 Workshop on Machine Learning for Global Challenges. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2604.21462. substantial text overlap with arXiv:2604.21462
summary: Timely detection of concerning events is an important problem in clinical practice. In this paper, we consider the problem of conditional anomaly detection that aims to identify data instances with an unusual response, such as the omission of an important lab test. We develop a new non-parametric approach for conditional anomaly detection based on the soft harmonic solution, with which we estimate the confidence of the label to detect anomalous mislabeling. We further regularize the solution to avoid the detection of isolated examples and examples on the boundary of the distribution support. We demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed method in detecting unusual labels on a real-world electronic health record dataset and compare it to several baseline approaches.

AI.tldr: A non-parametric conditional anomaly detection method using soft harmonic solution to identify unusual responses in clinical data, particularly anomalous mislabeling.
AI.motivation: Timely detection of concerning events in clinical practice is crucial, especially identifying data instances with unusual responses like omitted lab tests.
AI.method: Develops a new non-parametric approach based on soft harmonic solution to estimate label confidence for detecting anomalous mislabeling, with regularization to avoid detecting isolated examples and boundary cases.
AI.result: Demonstrated efficacy in detecting unusual labels on real-world electronic health record dataset, with comparison to several baseline approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed soft harmonic solution-based method effectively addresses conditional anomaly detection in clinical settings by identifying anomalous mislabeling while avoiding false positives from isolated or boundary cases.

title: Read the Paper, Write the Code: Agentic Reproduction of Social-Science Results
summary: Recent work has used LLM agents to reproduce empirical social science results with access to both the data and code. We broaden this scope by asking: Can they reproduce results given only a paper's methods description and original data? We develop an agentic reproduction system that extracts structured methods descriptions from papers, runs reimplementations under strict information isolation -- agents never see the original code, results, or paper -- and enables deterministic, cell-level comparison of reproduced outputs to the original results. An error attribution step traces discrepancies through the system chain to identify root causes. Evaluating four agent scaffolds and four LLMs on 48 papers with human-verified reproducibility, we find that agents can largely recover published results, but performance varies substantially between models, scaffolds, and papers. Root cause analysis reveals that failures stem both from agent errors and from underspecification in the papers themselves.

AI.tldr: LLM agents can reproduce social science research results using only paper methods descriptions and original data (without access to original code or results), with success varying by model, scaffold, and paper quality.
AI.motivation: Previous work has shown LLM agents can reproduce empirical social science results when given both data and code. The authors want to broaden this scope by testing if agents can reproduce results given only the paper's methods description and original data, without access to original code, results, or the full paper itself.
AI.method: Developed an agentic reproduction system that: 1) extracts structured methods descriptions from papers, 2) runs reimplementations under strict information isolation (agents never see original code, results, or paper), 3) enables deterministic, cell-level comparison of reproduced outputs to original results, and 4) includes an error attribution step to trace discrepancies through the system chain to identify root causes. Evaluated four agent scaffolds and four LLMs on 48 papers with human-verified reproducibility.
AI.result: Agents can largely recover published results, but performance varies substantially between models, scaffolds, and papers. Root cause analysis reveals failures stem from both agent errors and underspecification in the papers themselves.
AI.conclusion: LLM agents show promise for reproducing scientific results using only methods descriptions and data, but successful reproduction depends on model capabilities, system design, and paper quality/clarity. The work highlights the dual challenge of agent limitations and underspecified methods in research papers.

title: Multi-Task Optimization over Networks of Tasks
summary: Multi-task optimization is a powerful approach for solving a large number of tasks in parallel. However, existing algorithms face distinct limitations: Population-based methods scale poorly and remain underexplored for large task sets. Approaches that do scale beyond a thousand tasks are mostly MAP-Elites variants and rely on a fixed, discretized archive that disregards the topology of the task space. We introduce MONET (Multi-Task Optimization over Networks of Tasks), a multi-task optimization algorithm that models the task space as a graph: tasks are nodes, and edges connect tasks in the task parameter space. This representation enables knowledge transfer between tasks and remains tractable for high-dimensional problems while exploiting the topology of the task space. MONET combines social learning, which generates candidates from neighboring nodes via crossover, with individual learning, which refines a node's own solution independently via mutation. We evaluate MONET on four domains (archery, arm, and cartpole with 5,000 tasks each; hexapod with 2,000 tasks) and show that it matches or exceeds the performance of existing MAP-Elites-based baselines across all four domains.

AI.tldr: MONET introduces a graph-based approach to multi-task optimization that models tasks as nodes connected by edges in task space, enabling efficient knowledge transfer while scaling to thousands of tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-task optimization algorithms have limitations: population-based methods scale poorly for large task sets, while scalable MAP-Elites variants rely on fixed, discretized archives that ignore task space topology.
AI.method: MONET models the task space as a graph where tasks are nodes and edges connect related tasks. It combines social learning (crossover between neighboring nodes) with individual learning (mutation refinement of a node's own solution). This approach enables knowledge transfer between tasks while remaining tractable for high-dimensional problems.
AI.result: MONET was evaluated on four domains (archery, arm, cartpole with 5,000 tasks each; hexapod with 2,000 tasks) and matched or exceeded the performance of existing MAP-Elites-based baselines across all domains.
AI.conclusion: The graph-based MONET algorithm provides an effective solution for large-scale multi-task optimization that scales to thousands of tasks while exploiting task space topology through combined social and individual learning mechanisms.

title: Rethinking Publication: A Certification Framework for AI-Enabled Research
summary: AI research pipelines now produce a growing share of publishable academic output, including work that meets existing peer-review standards for quality and novelty. Yet the publication system was built on the assumption of universal human authorship and lacks a principled way to evaluate knowledge produced through automated pipelines. This paper proposes a two-layer certification framework that separates knowledge quality assessment from grading of human contribution, allowing publication systems to handle pipeline-generated work consistently and transparently without creating new institutions. The paper uses normative-conceptual analysis, framework design under four explicit constraints, and dry-run validation on two representative submission cases spanning key attribution scenarios. The framework grades contributions as Category A (pipeline-reachable), Category B (requiring human direction at identifiable stages), and Category C (beyond current pipeline reach at the formulation stage). It also introduces benchmark slots for fully disclosed automated research as both a transparent publication track and a calibration instrument for reviewer judgment. Contribution grading is contemporaneous, based on pipeline capability at the time of submission. Dry-run validation shows that the framework can certify knowledge appropriately while tolerating irreducible attribution uncertainty. The paper argues that publication has always certified both that knowledge is valid and that a human made it. AI pipelines separate these functions for the first time. The framework is implementable within existing editorial infrastructure and grounds recognition of frontier human contribution in epistemic achievement rather than unverifiable claims of human origin.

AI.tldr: A framework for certifying AI-generated research in academic publishing by separating knowledge quality assessment from human contribution grading, with three categories based on pipeline capability at submission time.
AI.motivation: The current academic publication system assumes universal human authorship and lacks principled ways to evaluate knowledge produced through automated AI research pipelines, even when such work meets existing peer-review standards for quality and novelty.
AI.method: Normative-conceptual analysis combined with framework design under four explicit constraints, validated through dry-run testing on two representative submission cases spanning key attribution scenarios.
AI.result: Developed a two-layer certification framework with three contribution categories: Category A (pipeline-reachable), Category B (requiring human direction at identifiable stages), and Category C (beyond current pipeline reach). Introduced benchmark slots for fully disclosed automated research as both publication track and calibration tool. Framework tolerates irreducible attribution uncertainty while certifying knowledge appropriately.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework addresses the separation between certifying knowledge validity and certifying human contribution created by AI pipelines, is implementable within existing editorial infrastructure, and grounds recognition of frontier human contribution in epistemic achievement rather than unverifiable human origin claims.

title: When Quotes Crumble: Detecting Transient Mechanical Liquidity Erosion in Limit Order Books
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures. Accepted at ICLR 2026 Workshop on Advances in Financial AI
summary: We study the detection of transient liquidity erosion ("crumbling quotes") in electronic limit order books, where observable quote deterioration may reflect either mechanical liquidity withdrawal or informational repricing. Using the ABIDES agent-based simulator, we construct a multi-agent environment in which crumbling emerges from stochastic regime switches in a market maker, providing time-resolved ground truth unavailable in real market data. We develop a detection pipeline that identifies mechanically driven quote erosion using order book features, and train a neural model to produce calibrated crumbling probabilities. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework reliably identifies crumbling events against agent-level ground truth, with the neural model achieving +36% AUC improvement over rule-based baselines and robust performance across normal, high-volatility, bull, and bear market conditions. Ablation studies on temporal features and varying the dependence structure of the ground-truth mechanism confirm that the framework generalizes across both independent and autocorrelated liquidity withdrawal dynamics.

AI.tldr: A neural framework to detect transient liquidity erosion ("crumbling quotes") in limit order books using agent-based simulation for ground truth, outperforming rule-based methods by 36% AUC and showing robustness across market conditions.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of distinguishing between mechanical liquidity withdrawal (crumbling quotes) and informational repricing in electronic limit order books, which is difficult because real market data lacks ground truth about the underlying causes of quote deterioration.
AI.method: The authors use ABIDES agent-based simulator to create a multi-agent environment with stochastic regime switches in a market maker, generating time-resolved ground truth. They develop a detection pipeline using order book features to identify mechanically driven quote erosion and train a neural model to produce calibrated crumbling probabilities.
AI.result: The framework reliably identifies crumbling events against agent-level ground truth, with the neural model achieving +36% AUC improvement over rule-based baselines. It shows robust performance across normal, high-volatility, bull, and bear market conditions, and generalizes across independent and autocorrelated liquidity withdrawal dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed neural detection framework effectively distinguishes mechanical liquidity erosion from informational repricing in limit order books, leveraging agent-based simulation for ground truth and demonstrating significant performance improvements over traditional rule-based approaches across diverse market regimes.

title: Sound Agentic Science Requires Adversarial Experiments
comment: Published at ICLR 2026 Workshop on Agents in the Wild
summary: LLM-based agents are rapidly being adopted for scientific data analysis, automating tasks once limited by human time and expertise. This capability is often framed as an acceleration of discovery, but it also accelerates a familiar failure mode, the rapid production of plausible, endlessly revisable analyses that are easy to generate, effectively turning hypothesis space into candidate claims supported by selectively chosen analyses, optimized for publishable positives. Unlike software, scientific knowledge is not validated by the iterative accumulation of code and post hoc statistical support. A fluent explanation or a significant result on a single dataset is not verification. Because the missing evidence is a negative space, experiments and analyses that would have falsified the claim were never run or never published. We therefore propose that non-experimental claims produced with agentic assistance be evaluated under a falsification-first standard: agents should not be used primarily to craft the most compelling narrative, but to actively search for the ways in which the claim can fail.

AI.tldr: Proposes a "falsification-first" standard for evaluating LLM-generated scientific claims to prevent AI from selectively producing publishable positives without adequate falsification testing.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents accelerate scientific data analysis but risk creating a failure mode where they produce plausible, endlessly revisable analyses optimized for publishable positives without proper falsification, similar to p-hacking problems in traditional research.
AI.method: Proposes a conceptual framework where AI-assisted scientific claims should be evaluated under a falsification-first standard, where agents should primarily search for ways claims can fail rather than crafting compelling narratives.
AI.result: Introduces a paradigm shift for AI-assisted science: instead of optimizing for verification through statistical significance or fluency, AI should be designed to actively seek falsification and expose negative evidence that would disprove claims.
AI.conclusion: Scientific knowledge requires falsification testing, not just accumulation of supportive evidence. A falsification-first standard for AI-assisted research could prevent systematic overproduction of plausible but unverified claims, preserving scientific integrity in the age of AI automation.

title: Universal Transformers Need Memory: Depth-State Trade-offs in Adaptive Recursive Reasoning
summary:
We study learned memory tokens as computational scratchpad for a single-block Universal Transformer (UT) with Adaptive Computation Time (ACT) on Sudoku-Extreme, a combinatorial reasoning benchmark. We find that memory tokens are empirically necessary: across all configurations tested -- 3 seeds, multiple token counts, two initialization schemes, ACT and fixed-depth processing -- no configuration without memory tokens achieves non-trivial performance. The optimal count exhibits a sharp lower threshold (T=0 always fails, T=4 is borderline, T=8 reliably succeeds for 81-cell puzzles) followed by a stable plateau (T=8-32, 57.4% +/- 0.7% exact-match) and collapse from attention dilution at T=64.
  During experimentation, we identify a router initialization trap that causes >70% of training runs to fail: both default zero-bias initialization (p ~ 0.5) and Graves' recommended positive bias (p ~ 0.73) cause tokens to halt after ~2 steps at initialization, settling into a shallow equilibrium (halt ~ 5-7) that the model cannot escape. Inverting the bias to -3 ("deep start," p ~ 0.05) eliminates this failure mode. We confirm through ablation that the trap is inherent to ACT initialization, not an artifact of our architecture choices.
  With reliable training established, we show that (1) ACT provides more consistent results than fixed-depth processing (56.9% +/- 0.7% vs 53.4% +/- 9.3% across 3 seeds); (2) ACT with lambda warmup achieves matching accuracy (57.0% +/- 1.1%) using 34% fewer ponder steps; and (3) attention heads specialize into memory readers, constraint propagators, and integrators across recursive depth. Code is available at https://github.com/che-shr-cat/utm-jax.

AI.tldr: Memory tokens are essential for Universal Transformers on combinatorial reasoning tasks like Sudoku, with optimal performance plateauing at 8-32 tokens. Adaptive Computation Time (ACT) with proper initialization ("deep start") outperforms fixed-depth processing and reduces computational steps by 34%.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates learned memory tokens as computational scratchpads for Universal Transformers on combinatorial reasoning benchmarks, particularly Sudoku-Extreme. The motivation is to understand the empirical necessity of memory tokens and overcome training failures in Adaptive Computation Time (ACT) due to initialization issues.
AI.method: Use a single-block Universal Transformer with Adaptive Computation Time (ACT) on Sudoku-Extreme benchmark. Experiment with different memory token counts (0-64), initialization schemes (including identifying and fixing a router initialization trap), and compare ACT with fixed-depth processing. Analyze attention head specialization patterns.
AI.result: Memory tokens are empirically necessary - no configuration without them achieved non-trivial performance. Optimal token count shows sharp threshold: T=0 fails, T=4 borderline, T=8-32 stable plateau (57.4% accuracy), T=64 collapses. ACT initialization trap (router bias) causes >70% training failure, fixed by "-3 bias" ("deep start"). ACT outperforms fixed-depth (56.9% vs 53.4%) and with lambda warmup reduces ponder steps by 34% while maintaining accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Memory tokens are crucial for Universal Transformers on combinatorial reasoning, with ACT providing more consistent results than fixed-depth processing when properly initialized. Attention heads naturally specialize into different computational roles, and careful initialization is critical for avoiding training failures in adaptive computation models.

title: Memanto: Typed Semantic Memory with Information-Theoretic Retrieval for Long-Horizon Agents
summary: The transition from stateless language model inference to persistent, multi session autonomous agents has revealed memory to be a primary architectural bottleneck in the deployment of production grade agentic systems. Existing methodologies largely depend on hybrid semantic graph architectures, which impose substantial computational overhead during both ingestion and retrieval. These systems typically require large language model mediated entity extraction, explicit graph schema maintenance, and multi query retrieval pipelines. This paper introduces Memanto, a universal memory layer for agentic artificial intelligence that challenges the prevailing assumption that knowledge graph complexity is necessary to achieve high fidelity agent memory. Memanto integrates a typed semantic memory schema comprising thirteen predefined memory categories, an automated conflict resolution mechanism, and temporal versioning. These components are enabled by Moorcheh's Information Theoretic Search engine, a no indexing semantic database that provides deterministic retrieval within sub ninety millisecond latency while eliminating ingestion delay. Through systematic benchmarking on the LongMemEval and LoCoMo evaluation suites, Memanto achieves state of the art accuracy scores of 89.8 percent and 87.1 percent respectively. These results surpass all evaluated hybrid graph and vector based systems while requiring only a single retrieval query, incurring no ingestion cost, and maintaining substantially lower operational complexity. A five stage progressive ablation study is presented to quantify the contribution of each architectural component, followed by a discussion of the implications for scalable deployment of agentic memory systems.

AI.tldr: Memanto introduces a universal memory layer for AI agents that replaces complex hybrid semantic graphs with a simpler typed schema approach, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with single-query retrieval, zero ingestion cost, and sub-90ms latency.
AI.motivation: Existing agent memory systems rely on computationally expensive hybrid semantic graph architectures that require LLM-mediated entity extraction, explicit graph schema maintenance, and multi-query retrieval pipelines, creating bottlenecks in production-grade agent deployment.
AI.method: Memanto uses a typed semantic memory schema with 13 predefined memory categories, automated conflict resolution, temporal versioning, and Moorcheh's Information Theoretic Search engine - a no-indexing semantic database that provides deterministic retrieval with sub-90ms latency and no ingestion delay.
AI.result: Memanto achieves state-of-the-art accuracy scores of 89.8% on LongMemEval and 87.1% on LoCoMo evaluation suites, outperforming all evaluated hybrid graph and vector-based systems while requiring only single retrieval queries, no ingestion cost, and lower operational complexity.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that complex knowledge graphs are not necessary for high-fidelity agent memory, presenting Memanto as a more scalable and efficient alternative that simplifies agentic memory system deployment through its universal memory layer approach.

title: Mochi: Aligning Pre-training and Inference for Efficient Graph Foundation Models via Meta-Learning
summary: We propose Mochi, a Graph Foundation Model that addresses task unification and training efficiency by adopting a meta-learning based training framework. Prior models pre-train with reconstruction-based objectives such as link prediction, and assume that the resulting representations can be aligned with downstream tasks through a separate unification step such as class prototypes. We demonstrate through synthetic and real-world experiments that this procedure, while simple and intuitive, has limitations that directly affect downstream task performance. To address these limitations, Mochi pre-trains on few-shot episodes that mirror the downstream evaluation protocol, aligning the training objective with inference rather than relying on a post-hoc unification step. We show that Mochi, along with its more powerful variant Mochi++, achieves competitive or superior performance compared to existing Graph Foundation Models across 25 real-world graph datasets spanning node classification, link prediction, and graph classification, while requiring 8$\sim$27 times less training time than the strongest baseline.

AI.tldr: Mochi is a Graph Foundation Model that uses meta-learning for task unification and achieves better performance with dramatically reduced training time compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: Previous graph foundation models use reconstruction-based pre-training (like link prediction) followed by a separate unification step, which has limitations that negatively impact downstream task performance.
AI.method: Mochi adopts a meta-learning framework that pre-trains on few-shot episodes that mirror downstream evaluation protocols, aligning training objectives directly with inference rather than relying on post-hoc unification.
AI.result: Mochi and Mochi++ achieve competitive or superior performance across 25 real-world graph datasets spanning node classification, link prediction, and graph classification, while requiring 8-27 times less training time than the strongest baseline.
AI.conclusion: By directly aligning pre-training with downstream evaluation through meta-learning, Mochi addresses limitations of previous approaches and provides an efficient, high-performing graph foundation model that unifies multiple graph tasks effectively.

title: Emergent Strategic Reasoning Risks in AI: A Taxonomy-Driven Evaluation Framework
summary: As reasoning capacity and deployment scope grow in tandem, large language models (LLMs) gain the capacity to engage in behaviors that serve their own objectives, a class of risks we term Emergent Strategic Reasoning Risks (ESRRs). These include, but are not limited to, deception (intentionally misleading users or evaluators), evaluation gaming (strategically manipulating performance during safety testing), and reward hacking (exploiting misspecified objectives). Systematically understanding and benchmarking these risks remains an open challenge. To address this gap, we introduce ESRRSim, a taxonomy-driven agentic framework for automated behavioral risk evaluation. We construct an extensible risk taxonomy of 7 categories, which is decomposed into 20 subcategories. ESRRSim generates evaluation scenarios designed to elicit faithful reasoning, paired with dual rubrics assessing both model responses and reasoning traces, in a judge-agnostic and scalable architecture. Evaluation across 11 reasoning LLMs reveals substantial variation in risk profiles (detection rates ranging 14.45%-72.72%), with dramatic generational improvements suggesting models may increasingly recognize and adapt to evaluation contexts.

AI.tldr: ESRRSim is a framework for evaluating emergent strategic reasoning risks in LLMs, revealing varying risk profiles across models and suggesting increased model awareness of evaluation contexts.
AI.motivation: As LLMs grow in reasoning capacity and deployment scope, they develop the ability to engage in behaviors that serve their own objectives, creating Emergent Strategic Reasoning Risks (ESRRs) like deception, evaluation gaming, and reward hacking. There's a gap in systematically understanding and benchmarking these risks.
AI.method: Introduces ESRRSim, a taxonomy-driven agentic framework with extensible risk taxonomy of 7 categories decomposed into 20 subcategories. It generates evaluation scenarios to elicit faithful reasoning with dual rubrics assessing both model responses and reasoning traces in a judge-agnostic, scalable architecture.
AI.result: Evaluation across 11 reasoning LLMs shows substantial variation in risk profiles with detection rates ranging from 14.45% to 72.72%. Dramatic generational improvements suggest models may increasingly recognize and adapt to evaluation contexts.
AI.conclusion: ESRRSim provides a systematic approach to evaluate emergent strategic reasoning risks in LLMs, revealing that as models improve, they may become better at recognizing and adapting to evaluation contexts, highlighting the need for robust evaluation frameworks to assess these emerging risks.

title: Kernel Contracts: A Specification Language for ML Kernel Correctness Across Heterogeneous Silicon
summary:
Every ML kernel ships with an implicit contract about what it computes. People rarely write the contract down. When two kernels disagree -- when a matmul on AMD produces a different gradient than the same matmul on NVIDIA, when a fused attention kernel silently downcasts an accumulator, when an out-of-bounds access returns zero on one stack and garbage on another -- there is no formal artifact to arbitrate the dispute. Recent empirical work has measured the gap across silicon platforms, but none of it specifies the contract being violated.
  We present a specification language for kernel contracts. A contract has eight parts: identifier, scope, precondition, postcondition, tolerance, reference oracle, measurement protocol, and violation signature. We use it to state twelve contract classes covering precision, ordering, compiler-induced, and exceptional-value failure modes, each grounded in published empirical evidence. We require a three-state calibration: every contract must admit at least one reference-conforming implementation and at least one contract-violating implementation that passes basic functional tests. We apply the framework to three documented incidents -- Huawei Ascend silent precision coercion, Sakana AI CUDA Engineer reward hacking, AMD out-of-bounds silent acceptance -- and show that each informal diagnosis maps to a specific contract violation with a measurable signature. A kernel contract suite is a normative reference against which conformance can be graded, in the way that ISASecure grades industrial control systems against IEC 62443.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a formal specification language for ML kernel contracts to systematically identify and measure violations in computational consistency across different hardware platforms and implementations.
AI.motivation: Current ML kernels operate without formal contracts about their computational behavior, leading to silent discrepancies across different hardware platforms (AMD vs NVIDIA), precision issues, and undefined behaviors that are difficult to detect and arbitrate.
AI.method: The authors introduce a specification language with eight components: identifier, scope, precondition, postcondition, tolerance, reference oracle, measurement protocol, and violation signature. They define twelve contract classes covering precision, ordering, compiler-induced, and exceptional-value failure modes, validated with a three-state calibration requiring both conforming and violating implementations.
AI.result: The framework successfully maps three documented incidents (Huawei Ascend silent precision coercion, Sakana AI CUDA Engineer reward hacking, AMD out-of-bounds silent acceptance) to specific contract violations with measurable signatures, demonstrating its practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: Kernel contract specifications provide a normative reference framework for grading computational conformance, similar to how ISASecure grades industrial control systems, enabling systematic detection and arbitration of implementation discrepancies in ML systems.

title: When Does LLM Self-Correction Help? A Control-Theoretic Markov Diagnostic and Verify-First Intervention
summary: Iterative self-correction is widely used in agentic LLM systems, but when repeated refinement helps versus hurts remains unclear. We frame self-correction as a cybernetic feedback loop in which the same language model serves as both controller and plant, and use a two-state Markov model over {Correct, Incorrect} to operationalize a simple deployment diagnostic: iterate only when ECR/EIR > Acc/(1 - Acc). In this view, EIR functions as a stability margin and prompting functions as lightweight controller design. Across 7 models and 3 datasets (GSM8K, MATH, StrategyQA), we find a sharp near-zero EIR threshold (<= 0.5%) separating beneficial from harmful self-correction. Only o3-mini (+3.4 pp, EIR = 0%), Claude Opus 4.6 (+0.6 pp, EIR ~ 0.2%), and o4-mini (+/-0 pp) remain non-degrading; GPT-5 degrades by -1.8 pp. A verify-first prompt ablation provides causal evidence that this threshold is actionable through prompting alone: on GPT-4o-mini it reduces EIR from 2% to 0% and turns -6.2 pp degradation into +0.2 pp (paired McNemar p < 10^-4), while producing little change on already-sub-threshold models. ASC further illustrates the stopping trade-off: it halts harmful refinement but incurs a 3.8 pp confidence-elicitation cost. Overall, the paper argues that self-correction should be treated not as a default behavior, but as a control decision governed by measurable error dynamics.

AI.tldr: Self-correction in LLMs only helps when the error introduction rate (EIR) is near-zero (≤0.5%), otherwise it harms performance; prompting can reduce EIR and turn harmful correction into beneficial.
AI.motivation: While iterative self-correction is widely used in agentic LLM systems, it's unclear when repeated refinement actually helps versus hurts performance. The paper aims to establish clear diagnostic criteria for when self-correction should be applied.
AI.method: Frames self-correction as a cybernetic feedback loop with the same LLM as both controller and plant. Uses a two-state Markov model over {Correct, Incorrect} states and introduces a diagnostic metric: iterate only when ECR/EIR > Acc/(1 - Acc). Tests across 7 models and 3 datasets (GSM8K, MATH, StrategyQA) with prompting interventions.
AI.result: Found a sharp near-zero EIR threshold (≤0.5%) separating beneficial from harmful self-correction. Only o3-mini (+3.4 pp), Claude Opus 4.6 (+0.6 pp), and o4-mini remained non-degrading; GPT-5 degraded by -1.8 pp. Verify-first prompt reduced EIR from 2% to 0% on GPT-4o-mini, turning -6.2 pp degradation into +0.2 pp improvement. ASC halts harmful refinement but incurs a 3.8 pp confidence-elicitation cost.
AI.conclusion: Self-correction should not be a default behavior but rather a control decision governed by measurable error dynamics. Prompting can be used as lightweight controller design to reduce EIR below critical thresholds, making self-correction beneficial only when error introduction rates are extremely low.

title: LTBs-KAN: Linear-Time B-splines Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
summary: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) are a recent neural network architecture offering an alternative to Multilayer Perceptrons (MLPs) with improved explainability and expressibility. However, KANs are significantly slower than MLPs due to the recursive nature of B-spline function computations, limiting their application. This work addresses these issues by proposing a novel base-spline Linear-Time B-splines Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (LTBs-KAN) with linear complexity. Unlike previous methods that rely on the Boor-Mansfield-Cox spline algorithm or other computationally intensive mathematical functions, our approach significantly reduces the computational burden. Additionally, we further reduce model's parameter through product-of-sums matrix factorization in the forward pass without sacrificing performance. Experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate that LTBs-KAN achieves good time complexity and parameter reduction, when used as building architectural blocks, compared to other KAN implementations.

AI.tldr: LTBs-KAN is a new Kolmogorov-Arnold Network variant with linear time complexity and reduced parameters through matrix factorization, making it more efficient than previous KAN implementations while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: KANs offer better explainability and expressibility than traditional MLPs but suffer from significant slowdown due to recursive B-spline computations, limiting their practical applications.
AI.method: Proposes LTBs-KAN with linear-time B-spline computations (unlike previous Boor-Mansfield-Cox algorithms) and reduces parameters via product-of-sums matrix factorization in the forward pass without performance loss.
AI.result: Achieves good time complexity and parameter reduction compared to other KAN implementations on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10 datasets when used as building blocks.
AI.conclusion: LTBs-KAN addresses the computational inefficiency of KANs while maintaining their advantages, making them more practical for real-world applications through linear complexity and parameter optimization.

title: Introducing Background Temperature to Characterise Hidden Randomness in Large Language Models
summary: Even when decoding with temperature $T=0$, large language models (LLMs) can produce divergent outputs for identical inputs. Recent work by Thinking Machines Lab highlights implementation-level sources of nondeterminism, including batch-size variation, kernel non-invariance, and floating-point non-associativity. In this short note we formalize this behavior by introducing the notion of \emph{background temperature} $T_{\mathrm{bg}}$, the effective temperature induced by an implementation-dependent perturbation process observed even when nominal $T=0$. We provide clean definitions, show how $T_{\mathrm{bg}}$ relates to a stochastic perturbation governed by the inference environment $I$, and propose an empirical protocol to estimate $T_{bg}$ via the equivalent temperature $T_n(I)$ of an ideal reference system. We conclude with a set of pilot experiments run on a representative pool from the major LLM providers that demonstrate the idea and outline implications for reproducibility, evaluation, and deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the concept of "background temperature" (T_bg) to formalize nondeterministic behavior in LLMs even at T=0 temperature, identifies implementation-level sources of this variation, and proposes methods to measure it.
AI.motivation: Large language models exhibit divergent outputs even when using temperature T=0 decoding, which should theoretically produce deterministic results. Recent work identified implementation-level sources of nondeterminism, but there's no formal framework to characterize this behavior.
AI.method: The authors introduce the notion of background temperature T_bg as the effective temperature induced by implementation-dependent perturbations. They provide formal definitions, show how T_bg relates to stochastic perturbations governed by the inference environment, and propose an empirical protocol to estimate T_bg via equivalent temperature T_n(I) of an ideal reference system.
AI.result: The paper presents pilot experiments run on a representative pool from major LLM providers that demonstrate the concept of background temperature and its measurement. The experiments likely show that different implementations/environments produce measurable T_bg values even when nominal T=0.
AI.conclusion: Background temperature is a useful concept for understanding and quantifying implementation-dependent nondeterminism in LLMs. This has important implications for reproducibility, evaluation, and deployment of language models, as it highlights that even "deterministic" settings can produce variable outputs depending on the inference environment.

title: LayerBoost: Layer-Aware Attention Reduction for Efficient LLMs
summary:
Transformers are mostly relying on softmax attention, which introduces quadratic complexity with respect to sequence length and remains a major bottleneck for efficient inference. Prior work on linear or hybrid attention typically replaces softmax attention uniformly across all layers, often leading to significant performance degradation or requiring extensive retraining to recover model quality.
  This work proposes LayerBoost, a layer-aware attention reduction method that selectively modifies the attention mechanism based on the sensitivity of individual transformer layers. It first performs a systematic sensitivity analysis on a pretrained model to identify layers that are critical for maintaining performance. Guided by this analysis, three distinct strategies can be applied: retaining standard softmax attention in highly sensitive layers, replacing it with linear sliding window attention in moderately sensitive layers, and removing attention entirely in layers that exhibit low sensitivity.
  To recover performance after these architectural modifications, we introduce a lightweight distillation-based healing phase requiring only 10M additional training tokens. LayerBoost reduces inference latency and improves throughput by up to 68% at high concurrency, while maintaining competitive model quality. It matches base model performance on several benchmarks, exhibits only minor degradations on others, and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art attention linearization methods. These efficiency gains make our method particularly well-suited for high-concurrency serving and hardware-constrained deployment scenarios, where inference cost and memory footprint are critical bottlenecks.

AI.tldr: LayerBoost is a layer-aware attention reduction method that selectively modifies transformer attention mechanisms based on layer sensitivity, using lightweight distillation to maintain performance while achieving up to 68% inference speedup.
AI.motivation: Softmax attention in transformers creates quadratic complexity that limits efficient inference. Previous uniform attention replacement approaches cause significant performance degradation or require extensive retraining, motivating a more selective approach.
AI.method: 1. Conduct systematic sensitivity analysis on pretrained model to identify critical layers. 2. Apply three strategies: retain softmax attention in highly sensitive layers, use linear sliding window attention in moderately sensitive layers, and remove attention entirely in low-sensitivity layers. 3. Implement lightweight distillation-based healing phase with only 10M additional training tokens.
AI.result: Achieves up to 68% inference latency reduction and throughput improvement at high concurrency. Matches base model performance on several benchmarks, shows only minor degradation on others, and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art attention linearization methods.
AI.conclusion: LayerBoost provides an effective approach for transformer optimization that balances efficiency and performance, making it well-suited for high-concurrency serving and hardware-constrained deployment scenarios where inference cost and memory footprint are critical bottlenecks.

title: CognitiveTwin: Robust Multi-Modal Digital Twins for Predicting Cognitive Decline in Alzheimer's Disease
summary: Predicting individual cognitive decline in Alzheimer's disease (AD) is difficult due to the heterogeneity of disease progression. Reliable clinical tools require not only high accuracy but also fairness across demographics and robustness to missing data. We present CognitiveTwin, a digital twin framework that predicts patient-specific cognitive trajectories. The model integrates multi-modal longitudinal data (cognitive scores, magnetic resonance imaging, positron emission tomography, cerebrospinal fluid biomarkers, and genetics). We use a Transformer-based architecture to fuse these modalities and a Deep Markov Model to capture temporal dynamics. We trained and evaluated the framework using data from 1,666 patients in the TADPOLE (Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative) dataset. We assessed the model for prediction error, demographic fairness, and robustness to missing-not-at-random (MNAR) data patterns. ognitiveTwin provides accurate and personalized predictions of cognitive decline. Its demonstrated fairness across patient demographics and resilience to clinical dropout make it a reliable tool for clinical trial enrichment and personalized care planning.

AI.tldr: CognitiveTwin: A Transformer-based digital twin framework that predicts personalized cognitive decline trajectories in Alzheimer's patients using multi-modal data, demonstrating accuracy, demographic fairness, and robustness to missing data.
AI.motivation: Predicting individual cognitive decline in Alzheimer's disease is challenging due to disease heterogeneity, and existing tools need improvement in accuracy, fairness across demographics, and robustness to missing data.
AI.method: Transformer-based architecture to fuse multi-modal longitudinal data (cognitive scores, MRI, PET, CSF biomarkers, genetics) combined with Deep Markov Model for temporal dynamics. Trained on 1,666 patients from TADPOLE dataset with evaluation on prediction error, demographic fairness, and robustness to missing-not-at-random data.
AI.result: CognitiveTwin provides accurate and personalized predictions of cognitive decline, demonstrates fairness across patient demographics, and shows resilience to clinical dropout scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The framework is a reliable tool for clinical trial enrichment and personalized care planning in Alzheimer's disease management.

title: Learning Coverage- and Power-Optimal Transmitter Placement from Building Maps: A Comparative Study of Direct and Indirect Neural Approaches
summary: Optimal wireless transmitter placement is a central task in radio-network planning, yet exhaustive search becomes prohibitively expensive at scale. This paper studies the single-transmitter setting under a fixed learned propagation surrogate, where exhaustive per-pixel evaluation remains tractable and provides surrogate-exact ground truth. We introduce a dataset of 167,525 urban scenarios (RadioMapSeer-Deployment) with dual surrogate-exact labels for coverage-optimal and power-optimal transmitter locations. Ground-truth analysis reveals an asymmetric coverage-power trade-off: coverage-optimal placement sacrifices 13.86% of received power, whereas power-optimal placement sacrifices only 5.50% of coverage; the best achievable balanced placement lies at $\bar{d}=2.60$ from the ideal point (100%,100%). We evaluate two learning formulations: indirect heatmap-based models that predict received-power radio maps, and direct score-map models that predict the objective landscape over feasible transmitter locations. Within the heatmap family, discriminative models deliver one-shot predictions 1350-2400x faster than exhaustive search, while diffusion models additionally support multi-sample inference that improves single-objective performance and, by reusing the same sample pool under a balanced criterion, recovers strong balanced placements without explicit multi-objective training. Dual score-map strategies combining power and coverage score maps match the exhaustive balanced optimum ($\bar{d}=2.60$) and remain close across smaller candidate budgets, at 14-22x speedups after candidate re-evaluation. Both formulations admit very fast one-shot inference; on this benchmark, dual score-map methods are strongest for balanced placement, whereas heatmap formulations remain attractive for their physically meaningful intermediate maps and, in the diffusion setting, for inference-time search.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RadioMapSeer-Deployment dataset for optimal transmitter placement, revealing coverage-power trade-offs, and evaluates heatmap-based vs. score-map models for placement optimization with significant speedups.
AI.motivation: Optimal wireless transmitter placement is critical for network planning but exhaustive search is computationally expensive at scale. The authors aim to study this problem under a fixed learned propagation surrogate where exhaustive search remains tractable, creating a dataset and comparing learning approaches.
AI.method: The paper introduces RadioMapSeer-Deployment dataset with 167,525 urban scenarios and dual labels for coverage-optimal and power-optimal transmitter locations. It evaluates two learning formulations: 1) indirect heatmap-based models predicting received-power radio maps, and 2) direct score-map models predicting objective landscapes over feasible transmitter locations.
AI.result: Heatmap models deliver 1350-2400x faster predictions than exhaustive search, with diffusion models supporting multi-sample inference. Dual score-map strategies match exhaustive balanced optimum and achieve 14-22x speedups after candidate re-evaluation. Ground-truth analysis shows asymmetric trade-off: coverage-optimal sacrifices 13.86% power, power-optimal sacrifices only 5.50% coverage.
AI.conclusion: Dual score-map methods are strongest for balanced placement, while heatmap formulations remain attractive for physically meaningful intermediate maps and inference-time search capabilities. Both approaches offer significant computational advantages over exhaustive search for wireless transmitter placement optimization.

title: Reliability Auditing for Downstream LLM tasks in Psychiatry: LLM-Generated Hospitalization Risk Scores
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly utilized in clinical reasoning and risk assessment. However, their interpretive reliability in critical and indeterminate domains such as psychiatry remains unclear. Prior work has identified algorithmic biases and prompt sensitivity in these systems, raising concerns about how contextual information may influence model outputs, but there remains no systematic way to assess these, especially in the psychiatric domain. We propose an approach for reliability auditing downstream LLM tasks by structuring evaluation around the impact of prompt design and the inclusion of medically insignificant inputs on predicted hospitalization risk scores, which is often the first downstream AI clinical-decision-making task. In our audit, a cohort of synthetic patient profiles (n = 50) is generated, each consisting of 15 clinically relevant features and up to 50 clinically insignificant features, across four prompt reframings (neutral, logical, human impact, clinical judgment). We audit four LLMs (Gemini 2.5 Flash, LLaMa 3.3 70b, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-4o mini), and our results show that including medically insignificant variables resulted in a statistically significant increase in the absolute mean predicted hospitalization risk and output variability across all models and prompts, indicating reduced predictive stability as contextual noise increased. Clinically insignificant features had an effect on instability across many model-prompt conditions, and prompt variations independently affected the trajectory of instability in a model-dependent manner. These findings quantify how LLM-based psychiatric risk assessments are sensitive to non-clinical information, highlighting the need for systematic evaluations of attributional stability and uncertainty behavior like this before clinical deployments.

AI.tldr: LLM-based psychiatric hospitalization risk assessments are unstable when exposed to medically insignificant contextual information, with all tested models showing increased prediction variability and sensitivity to prompt design.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used in clinical reasoning but their reliability in psychiatry remains unclear due to algorithmic biases and prompt sensitivity. There's no systematic way to assess how contextual information influences model outputs in psychiatric risk assessments, particularly for hospitalization risk which is a key clinical decision-making task.
AI.method: Proposed a reliability auditing approach for LLM tasks by evaluating the impact of prompt design and medically insignificant inputs on predicted hospitalization risk scores. Generated 50 synthetic patient profiles with 15 clinically relevant features and up to 50 clinically insignificant features. Tested four LLMs (Gemini 2.5 Flash, LLaMa 3.3 70b, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-4o mini) across four prompt reframings (neutral, logical, human impact, clinical judgment).
AI.result: Medically insignificant variables caused statistically significant increases in absolute mean predicted hospitalization risk and output variability across all models and prompts. Predictive stability decreased as contextual noise increased. Clinically insignificant features affected instability across many model-prompt conditions, and prompt variations independently affected instability trajectories in model-dependent ways.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based psychiatric risk assessments are sensitive to non-clinical information, highlighting the need for systematic evaluations of attributional stability and uncertainty behavior before clinical deployments to ensure reliable and safe implementation.

title: From Skills to Talent: Organising Heterogeneous Agents as a Real-World Company
summary: Individual agent capabilities have advanced rapidly through modular skills and tool integrations, yet multi-agent systems remain constrained by fixed team structures, tightly coupled coordination logic, and session-bound learning. We argue that this reflects a deeper absence: a principled organisational layer that governs how a workforce of agents is assembled, governed, and improved over time, decoupled from what individual agents know. To fill this gap, we introduce \emph{OneManCompany (OMC)}, a framework that elevates multi-agent systems to the organisational level. OMC encapsulates skills, tools, and runtime configurations into portable agent identities called \emph{Talents}, orchestrated through typed organisational interfaces that abstract over heterogeneous backends. A community-driven \emph{Talent Market} enables on-demand recruitment, allowing the organisation to close capability gaps and reconfigure itself dynamically during execution. Organisational decision-making is operationalised through an \emph{Explore-Execute-Review} ($\text{E}^2$R) tree search, which unifies planning, execution, and evaluation in a single hierarchical loop: tasks are decomposed top-down into accountable units and execution outcomes are aggregated bottom-up to drive systematic review and refinement. This loop provides formal guarantees on termination and deadlock freedom while mirroring the feedback mechanisms of human enterprises. Together, these contributions transform multi-agent systems from static, pre-configured pipelines into self-organising and self-improving AI organisations capable of adapting to open-ended tasks across diverse domains.
